# Summary of Analysis & Visualizations

The analysis conducted aimed to identify whether students who had programming experience should need extension opportunities, specifically because they perceived course difficulty to be lower than those who did not have prior experience. 

<img src="/personal_website/static/imgs/graph1.png" alt="Graph1"  width="500"/>

Firstly, graph #1 consistently showed that those who had been coding for longer found the course pace to be slower, difficulty to be lesser, and their understanding of the course to be greater than those who didn't. While the identified increases/decreases were consistent for pace and difficulty, note that those who had been programming for 1-2 years had a greater understanding of the course than those who had been programming for 2+ years, on average. This is the only discrepancy.

<img src="/personal_website/static/imgs/graph2.png" alt="Graph2"  width="500"/>

Secondly, graph #2 indicates that those who could recognize (which likely correlates to have programming experience in) more languages perceived class difficulty to be lesser than those who could recognize a fewer amount of languages. This is once again consistent with the idea that students with prior coding experience require extension activities.

<img src="/personal_website/static/imgs/graph3.png" alt="Graph3"  width="500"/>

Finally, graph #3 indicates that those who had taken AP Computer Science Principles in high school also found course difficulty to be significantly lower than those who did not. This indicates a need to consider offering credit for COMP110 to those who scored past a certain threshold on the AP CSP exam.

# Final Conclusions

As such, all the data analysis supports the idea that coders with prior experience find COMP110 to be easier than those without. It is thereby necessary for extension activities to be created in the course so even experienced programmers can continue to develop their skillset.

There is a major trade-off that results from implementing this idea, though. Course instructors would have to develop more activities/exercises for students to complete, creating more work for them. At the same time, all COMP110 TA's, who grade the assignments, would have to be equally experienced as the advanced members in this class to be able to grade the extension activities.

To refine this idea and address the trade-off, the extension activities offered to students who have prior experience could be peer-reviewed. A group of students can be made on Gradescope based upon how much experience they have, and projects can be rotated amongst them so extra responsibilities do not have to be put on COMP110 TA's or the instructor.
